skill.name: enact-hello-ruby
skill.description: Runs a small Ruby greeting tool through Enact and returns a personalized hello message.
skill.summary: Generate a Ruby-powered greeting from a provided name.
skill.author: EnactProtocol

security_audit.summary: The weak cryptography findings at SKILL.md lines 5 and 14 are false positives because those lines are description text only. The reported backtick issue at SKILL.md lines 32-34 is also a false positive because it is a fenced usage example, but the manifest command at line 23 interpolates user input into a command string and should be treated as a medium command-injection concern.

medium_findings.title: User Input Interpolated Into Command String
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md line 23 defines command: "ruby /workspace/hello.rb ${name}". If the Enact runtime executes this string through a shell, a crafted name value could alter the command.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command string clearly includes user-controlled input. Exploitability depends on Enact runtime argument handling, so this is a medium-confidence concern rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Weak Cryptography Static Findings
low_findings.description: SKILL.md lines 5 and 14 contain plain description fields, not cryptographic code or algorithm selection. No evidence found of weak cryptography in those lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are YAML descriptions only. There is no cryptographic API, hash function, cipher, key handling, or encoded payload present.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Backtick Execution Static Finding
low_findings.description: SKILL.md lines 32-34 are a fenced bash usage example that shows how to run the skill. They are documentation, not executable skill logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced text is inside a Markdown code fence under the Usage section. It does not execute during skill operation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Parameterized Shell Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The manifest command includes the ${name} parameter directly in the command string. Runtime-safe argument separation or input escaping should be verified before broad publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The parameter interpolation is visible in the manifest. The available files do not show whether the runtime escapes arguments safely.

content.value_statement: Developers need small examples to verify Enact tool execution. This skill runs a Ruby script that returns a personalized greeting and Ruby version output.
actual_capabilities: Accepts a name input for a greeting.
Runs a Ruby script in the configured container.
Demonstrates a minimal Enact skill manifest.
limitations: Only supports a simple name input.
Does not perform validation beyond the declared string schema.
Does not call external services or APIs.
Command interpolation should be reviewed before production use.

use_cases.title: Verify Enact Setup
use_cases.description: Run a small Ruby example to confirm the local Enact workflow is installed and working.

use_cases.title: Teach Skill Packaging
use_cases.description: Show how a Ruby script, input schema, output schema, and command fit together.

use_cases.title: Prototype Ruby Tools
use_cases.description: Use the greeting flow as a starting point for a more useful Ruby command tool.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Greeting
prompt_templates.prompt: Use enact-hello-ruby to greet Alice and explain the returned output.

prompt_templates.title: Default Input Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Run enact-hello-ruby without a custom name and summarize the default greeting behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Runtime Verification
prompt_templates.prompt: Use enact-hello-ruby with the name Morgan and report the greeting plus the Ruby version.

prompt_templates.title: Packaging Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review enact-hello-ruby as a minimal Enact Ruby skill and identify what to change before production use.

output_examples.output: A greeting for Alice, followed by the Ruby runtime version.
A default hello message and the Ruby version used by the container.

best_practices: Use simple names when testing the example.
Review command interpolation before accepting untrusted input.
Treat this as a learning sample, not a production workflow.
anti_patterns: Do not pass secrets as the name value.
Do not expose this command to untrusted users without validation.
Do not assume the sample includes production error handling.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It runs a Ruby script that prints a personalized greeting and the Ruby runtime version.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it connect to the internet?
faq.answer: No evidence found of network access in the reviewed files.

faq.question: Is it safe for production use?
faq.answer: It should be reviewed before production use because user input is interpolated into a command string.

faq.question: What input does it accept?
faq.answer: It accepts a single string value named name, with World as the default.

faq.question: Can it be extended?
faq.answer: Yes. It is a minimal Ruby example that can be expanded with validation, richer output, and safer argument handling.
